text: A 67-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe chest pain that radiates to his back. The pain started while resting. Blood pressure is 190/100 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the BACK + hypertension = Aortic dissection. BP difference between arms is classic. Dx: CT angiography. Do NOT give anticoagulants.

text: A 4-year-old child presents with a barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and mild respiratory distress that is worse at night. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis): barking (seal-like) cough + stridor + hoarseness. Caused by parainfluenza virus. Tx: Dexamethasone + racemic epinephrine if severe.

text: A 22-year-old male presents with sudden severe testicular pain while playing soccer. The testicle is high-riding and the cremasteric reflex is absent. What is the next best step?
rationale: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. Do NOT wait for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high. 6-hour window for salvage. Absent cremasteric reflex is most reliable sign.

text: A patient presents with irregularly irregular pulse, palpitations, and fatigue. ECG shows no identifiable P waves. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves. Most feared complication = embolic stroke. Manage with rate control + anticoagulation (DOAC preferred).

text: A 50-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe headache he describes as the worst headache of his life. He was exercising at the time. Neurologic exam is normal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Thunderclap headache = worst headache of life = SAH until proven otherwise. Dx: CT head first → if negative → LP for xanthochromia. Cause: ruptured berry aneurysm.

text: A 45-year-old obese woman presents with right upper quadrant pain after eating a fatty meal, fever, and nausea. Murphy sign is positive. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute cholecystitis: RUQ pain + fever + positive Murphy sign + triggered by fatty meal. Dx: Ultrasound (gallstones + thickened GB wall). Tx: IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, cholecystectomy.

text: A 5-year-old child presents with high fever, drooling, difficulty swallowing, and inspiratory stridor. He is sitting in a tripod position and looks toxic. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis: drooling + dysphagia + toxic appearance + tripod position. MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Secure airway FIRST. Do NOT examine throat. IV antibiotics after airway secured.

text: A 35-year-old man presents with sudden severe flank pain radiating to the groin, hematuria, and nausea. The pain is colicky. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Ureteric stone: classic colicky flank pain radiating to groin + hematuria. Dx: CT KUB (non-contrast). Tx: analgesia (NSAIDs) + IV fluids + tamsulosin. Stones <5mm usually pass spontaneously.

text: A 4-week-old male infant presents with projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feeding. He appears hungry after vomiting. A olive-shaped mass is palpable in the epigastrium. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pyloric stenosis: projectile non-bilious vomiting at 2-6 weeks + olive mass + metabolic alkalosis (hypochloremic). More common in firstborn males. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

text: A 54-year-old man presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. Serum lipase is elevated 3x upper limit of normal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back + lipase >3x ULN. Causes: Gallstones #1, Alcohol #2 (GET SMASHED). Tx: IV fluids first, NPO, analgesia.

text: A 68-year-old man presents with painless progressive jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and 12 kg weight loss over 3 months. He has no abdominal pain. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Painless jaundice + weight loss in elderly = pancreatic head cancer until proven otherwise. Courvoisier sign: palpable non-tender gallbladder. Dx: CT abdomen. CA 19-9 elevated.

text: A 40-year-old woman presents with symmetric joint pain and swelling involving MCP and PIP joints, with morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes. RF and anti-CCP are positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: RA: symmetric MCP/PIP (not DIP) + morning stiffness >1h + RF/anti-CCP positive. Tx: Methotrexate (DMARD) first-line. NSAIDs for symptoms. Anti-CCP is most specific.

text: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling of the first metatarsophalangeal joint (big toe) that started overnight. Uric acid is elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale: Gout: podagra (first MTP joint) + hyperuricemia + negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Acute tx: NSAIDs or colchicine. Prevention: allopurinol (urate-lowering therapy).

text: A 30-year-old woman presents with weight loss, palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, and diarrhea. Examination shows exophthalmos and diffuse goiter. Diagnosis?
rationale: Graves disease: most common cause of hyperthyroidism. Exophthalmos is pathognomonic. Low TSH + high T3/T4. Tx: Methimazole (first-line), radioactive iodine, or surgery.

text: A 45-year-old man presents with central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, purple abdominal striae, and hypertension. Fasting glucose is elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale: Cushing syndrome: excess cortisol → moon face + central obesity + purple striae + HTN + hyperglycemia. Most common cause: exogenous steroids. Endogenous: pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease).

text: A 35-year-old woman has persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating for 6 weeks. She denies suicidal ideation. First-line treatment?
rationale: MDD first-line: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine). Allow 4-6 weeks for response. Treat minimum 6-9 months after remission. Benzodiazepines not first-line.

text: A 20-year-old man presents with 8-month history of auditory hallucinations, believing he is being controlled by aliens, social withdrawal, and flat affect. Diagnosis?
rationale: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) + negative symptoms (flat affect, withdrawal) >6 months. Tx: atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine). Clozapine for treatment-resistant.

text: A 9-month-old infant presents with episodic severe crampy abdominal pain, drawing up legs, and vomiting. Stool is described as currant jelly. Abdomen is distended. Diagnosis?
rationale: Intussusception: classic triad = colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stool (blood + mucus). Most common 6mo-3yr. Dx: Ultrasound. Tx: Air enema reduction (if no perforation), surgery if failed.

text: A 60-year-old man presents with black tarry stools (melena) and lightheadedness. He takes ibuprofen daily for arthritis. Most likely source of bleeding?
rationale: Melena = digested blood = upper GI source (above ligament of Treitz). NSAID use → peptic ulcer = #1 cause of upper GI bleeding. Dx: Urgent endoscopy. Tx: PPI + endoscopic hemostasis.

text: A 72-year-old woman develops sudden right-sided weakness, right facial droop, and inability to speak. Symptoms started 2 hours ago. What is the most appropriate first investigation?
rationale: Acute stroke: CT head NON-CONTRAST first to exclude hemorrhage before tPA. MRI more sensitive but slower. Time is brain - door-to-needle <60 min. tPA window: 4.5 hours.
